leadAgency: Alabama Department of Economic and Community Affairs (ADECA)
notes: Alabama received $203.4M for FY2026. ADECA developing rules to help healthcare providers and eligible recipients 'apply and compete' for funding under 11 ARHTP initiatives. Governor Ivey signed executive order establishing Rural Health Transformation Advisory Group; first meeting held February 4, 2026. Advisory Group chaired by Sen. Chesteen with Rep. Kiel as vice chair. Group discussed processes, timelines, and potential legislative action. ADECA Director Kenneth Boswell presented program overview to the group and briefed lawmakers on spending plans. ADECA still searching for a consultant familiar with federal processes - once selected, hospitals can apply for funds aligned with initiatives. Entity-level funding expected to be distributed toward end of FY2026 (by Sept 30, 2026). Revised distribution plan resubmitted to CMS on Jan 30, 2026; CMS has 30 days to respond. Alabama Legislature in session (typically Feb-May). No vendor RFA/RFP posted yet. Program page shows only project narrative PDF; no implementation updates or timeline posted on ADECA website.
programAddress: Alabama Department of Economic and Community Affairs (ADECA), P.O. Box 5690, Montgomery, AL 36103-5690

leadAgency: Alaska Department of Health
notes: Alaska received $272.2M/year for 5 years. LOI REGISTRATION NOW OPEN: Letter of Interest portal opened February 17, 2026 and closes March 11, 2026, submitted via Alaska Community Foundation (ACF) Grant Lifecycle Manager portal. LOI helps DOH understand project ideas and organizational readiness; no attachments, detailed budgets, or letters of support required. LOIs reviewed for completeness and alignment to RHTP priorities. Office Hours webinars: Feb 17, Feb 24, March 3 (noon-1 PM). ACF is subrecipient administrator managing applications and fund distribution. Four funding pathways: (1) Administrative readiness grants, (2) Planning grants (expected April), (3) Project implementation grants (expected May), (4) Targeted innovation prepackaged projects. All applications require Unique Entity ID (UEI) via Sam.gov. Broad eligibility: Alaska providers, clinics, hospitals, non-profits, community organizations, tribal organizations. Sustainability plan required with all applications. DOH capped administrative spending at 10%. Multiple application windows tied to federal budget periods. Funding awards expected Spring 2026; additional opportunities in each funding year. Contact: RHTP@alaska.gov. Updates signup: public.govdelivery.com/accounts/AKDHSS/signup/48430.
programAddress: Alaska Department of Health, 3601 C Street, Suite 902, Anchorage, AK 99503

leadAgency: Arizona Center for Rural Health / AHCCCS / ADHS
notes: Arizona received $167.0M for FY2026. Revised 74-page narrative and budget submitted to CMS Jan 30, 2026; CMS has 30-day review period (approval expected by end of Feb/early March 2026). Arizona received $33M less than expected, requiring budget revisions. State toolkit page confirms NOFOs/RFPs likely starting March/April 2026 after CMS budget approval. AZ Public Health Association reported on Feb 9 that the state turned in revised grant proposal. AHCCCS is designated lead for administering/distributing funds - revised application shifts more oversight to AHCCCS for issuing/evaluating RFPs. AHCCCS RHTP page not yet live; no RHTP solicitations on AHCCCS Open Solicitations page as of Feb 27. Four priority areas with revised allocations: Rural Health Education & Training (28%, Office of Economic Opportunity), Priority Health Initiative Grants including Behavioral Health (16%, AHCCCS/ADHS), Making Rural Health Care Accessible (23%, AHCCCS), Making Rural Health Resilient for Medical Equipment & Technology (23%, AHCCCS). Arizona Center for Rural Health offers letters of support via online form. Monitor program toolkit page and AHCCCS solicitations page for NOFO release.
programAddress: Arizona Center for Rural Health, 1295 N. Martin Ave., P.O. Box 245163, Tucson, AZ 85724

leadAgency: Office of Governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders
notes: Arkansas received $208.8M for FY2026 ($209M rounded). Governor's office and DFA led the application. Four initiatives: RISE AR (workforce/residencies), THRIVE (AI/telehealth/coordinated records), HEART (preventive care/health outcomes), PACT (specialty care/screenings/trauma services). First disbursement of funding to the state expected early 2026. No downstream RFA, RFP, or subgrant application has been posted yet. ACHI (Arkansas Center for Health Improvement) published program overview at achi.net/publications/overview-of-the-rural-health-transformation-program-in-arkansas/.
programAddress: Office of Governor Sarah Huckabee Sanders, State Capitol, Suite 250, 500 Woodlane Street, Little Rock, AR 72201

leadAgency: California Department of Health Care Access and Information (HCAI)
notes: California received $233.6M for FY2026. HCAI/CalSORH is the lead. Still in planning phase - application narratives under CMS review and subject to change pending final CMS award decisions. Updated budget narrative posted January 2026. Three overarching initiative areas: Transformative Care Model, Workforce Development, Technology. Kickoff webinar (Sept 4, 2025) and five listening sessions (Oct 1-3, 2025) recordings available. Stakeholders encouraged to join Rural Health email listserv for updates on webinars, surveys, and engagement opportunities. Contact: CalSORH@hcai.ca.gov or CalRHT@hcai.ca.gov. RHTP Program Overview, Project Narrative, Budget Narrative, and Project Abstract available on program page.
programAddress: Department of Health Care Access and Information (HCAI), 2020 West El Camino Avenue, Suite 800, Sacramento, CA 95833

leadAgency: Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF)
notes: Colorado received $200.1M for FY2026. HCPF is actively developing RFA materials. RHTP Advisory Committee applications closed February 16, 2026; committee members to be announced soon. First Advisory Committee meeting: March 2026. Advisory Committee will advise HCPF on application process, funding priorities, and public-facing materials. Feb 11, 2026 Hospital Webinar held (7:30-10:00 AM MST) providing RHTP update and discussing hospital impacts from H.R.1 and federal policy changes. Application scoring/evaluation will be conducted by external vendor overseen by HCPF. Advisory Committee overview memo available on program page. RFA cannot be finalized until cooperative agreement with CMS is complete. 10 initiatives that providers/nonprofits can submit proposals to fulfill. Eligible providers in 52 rural/frontier Colorado counties and two federally recognized tribes should monitor for RFA release. Newsletters issued Jan 5 and Jan 12, 2026. Contact: hcpf_RHTP@state.co.us.
programAddress: Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF), 303 E. 17th Avenue, Suite 1100, Denver, CO 80203

leadAgency: Connecticut Department of Social Services (DSS)
notes: Connecticut received $154.2M for FY2026. State has NOT received CMS authorization to distribute funds - subject to additional negotiation and review. DSS has hired/is hiring three positions for RHTP implementation: Assistant Director of Rural Health Transformation (under DSS, posting closed Jan 6, 2026), and two Health Information Technology Durational Project Managers (under Office of Health Strategy, funded through Feb 2030). Documents page has Governor's endorsement letter, project narrative, and budget narrative. Public comment page available. 31 different initiatives including mobile medical/dental vans and expanded telehealth. When vendor applications are released, they will appear on both DSS RHTP page and CT state procurement website. Monitor both regularly.
programAddress: Connecticut Department of Social Services, 55 Farmington Avenue, Hartford, CT 06105

leadAgency: Delaware Department of Health and Social Services / Division of Public Health
notes: Delaware received $157.4M for FY2026. SIX RFPs NOW OPEN on Delaware's procurement portal (mmp.delaware.gov). Active RFPs posted Feb 6, 2026: (1) Medical School Branch Campus (HSS26062-MEDSCHPSHP — Bid 8988) - $42.5M, deadline March 27, 2026; (2) Food Is Medicine Infrastructure Initiative (HSS26060-FIMII — Bid 8980) - $1.65M, deadline April 10, 2026; (3) Rural Community Health Hubs (HSS26062-RURALCHHUB — Bid 8989) - $5.48M, deadline April 17, 2026; (4) School-Based Health Centers Expansion Initiative (HSS26064-SBHCEI — Bid 8990) - $195K, deadline April 10, 2026; (5) Healthy Living in Rural Delaware / Continuous Glucose Monitoring Program (HSS26063-HLIRDDWP — Bid 8996) - $950K, deadline April 16, 2026; (6) Rural Library-Based Health Access (HSS26065-RLIBBASHLT — Bid 8997) - $1.84M, deadline April 15, 2026. Additional RFPs expected on a rolling basis in spring 2026 for: Hope Centers ($26.4M), Telehealth & RPM ($5M), Workforce Data Technology ($2.7M), Value-Based Care Transformation ($24.3M), Prior Authorization Network ($6.4M), and others. Contact: ricky.williamson@delaware.gov or dhssinfo@delaware.gov.
programAddress: Office of Governor Matt Meyer, 150 Martin Luther King Jr Blvd South, Dover, DE 19901

leadAgency: Florida Agency for Health Care Administration (AHCA)
notes: Florida received $209.9M for FY2026. AHCA plans to release an RFA where regional collaboratives (groups of rural and other providers) will submit grant proposals. PREREQUISITE: AHCA must receive legislative appropriation before releasing funds/RFA - CMS also instructed all states that procurements cannot be released until revised budgets are reviewed and approved. Budget revisions submitted to CMS Jan 29, 2026. Florida Legislature's 2026 session considering rural renaissance legislation (SB 250, passed Senate unanimously with bipartisan support). Feb 18, 2026 webinar held - AHCA discussed program structure, timelines, and expectations; recording available on YouTube. AHCA FAQ updated Feb 2, 2026 available on program page. AHCA conducting statewide townhall series: completed in Jackson, Franklin, Okeechobee, Suwannee, Citrus (Feb 20), Nassau/Hilliard (Feb 27) counties; upcoming: Clewiston/Hendry (Mar 9), Arcadia/DeSoto (Mar 9), Wauchula/AdventHealth (Mar 10), Lakeland (Mar 10). Regional collaboratives must have partnerships established at time of application - executed MOUs/partnership agreements required. Multiple sub-awards anticipated across regions and initiatives. 15 key initiatives spanning: access to care, behavioral health, telehealth, tele-ICU, workforce, payment reform, RPM, nutrition/SDOH. Three core priorities: workforce development, technological infrastructure/innovation, increasing access to care. Monitor: ahca.myflorida.com/procurements and MyFloridaMarketPlace VIP portal. Contact: RHTP@ahca.myflorida.com.
programAddress: Agency for Health Care Administration (AHCA), 2727 Mahan Drive, Tallahassee, FL 32308

leadAgency: Georgia Department of Community Health (DCH)
notes: Georgia received $218.9M for FY2026 under 'GREAT Health' program. DCH received final CMS approval for amended Year 1 budget on February 26, 2026. DCH now coordinating with subgrantees (DPH, DBHDD, GA Board of Health Care Workforce), hiring 20 dedicated staff, engaging project management vendors, and launching strategic planning. RFGAs (Requests for Grant Applications) expected to open Spring 2026 to expand rural health collaboration and innovation. A more robust online hub for GREAT Health is in development for all future RFGAs, news, and materials. FIRST RHTP-FUNDED OPPORTUNITY: GA Board of Health Care Workforce GME Fellowship Expansion Grant - intent to increase Fellowship positions due by March 15, 2026; questions due March 1, 2026; approval notifications begin April 10, 2026 (rolling). Up to 12 Fellowship slots at $200K/PGY year for priority specialties (IM, FM, Peds, OB/GYN, Surgery, Geriatrics, Psych, EM, Neuro) in eligible rural hospitals under RHTP Value-Based Care Model. AHEAD model: 57 of 93 invited hospitals submitted LOIs, with March 15 deadline. DCH Grant Announcements page shows existing RFGA Dual Track FY26 - Rural Hospital Support Grant (posted Jan 20, not RHTP-funded). Funds must be allocated by October 2026. 29 separate projects across five major categories. Register for DCH grant announcements at grants@dch.ga.gov. Contact: GREATHealth@dch.ga.gov.
programAddress: Georgia Department of Community Health, 2 Martin Luther King Jr. Drive SE, East Tower, Atlanta, GA 30334

leadAgency: Executive Office of the State of Hawaii
notes: Hawaii received $188.9M for FY2026 (one of highest per-capita amounts). State finalizing cooperative agreement with CMS - must be completed before releasing funds. Six initiatives: Rural Health Information Network (RHIN), Pili Ola Telehealth Network, Rural Infrastructure for Care Access (RICA), HOME RUN Rural Healthcare Workforce Pipeline, Rural Respite Network, Rural Value-Based Innovation and AHEAD Readiness Fund. State will finalize updated budget based on $188.9M award following CMS negotiations, then begin releasing funds to lead agencies and partners. All procurements must follow state procurement laws; solicitations will appear on HIePRO (hiepro.ehawaii.gov) and HANDS (hands.ehawaii.gov/hands/opportunities). No RHTP-specific procurements on HIePRO or SPO RFP page yet. Monitor: HIePRO and SPO Request for Proposals page (spo.hawaii.gov).
programAddress: Executive Chambers, State Capitol, 415 South Beretania Street, Honolulu, HI 96813

leadAgency: Idaho Department of Health and Welfare (DHW)
notes: Idaho received $186.0M annually for five years (~$930M total). Funding will be subawarded in accordance with Idaho procurement law. Subaward solicitations will include criteria such as full pricing transparency to patients, rural access guarantees, long-term fiscal discipline and sustainability planning, alignment with target populations, feasibility within the funding period, and direct health impact on rural Idahoans. Contracts and partnerships begin executing in Q1-Q2 of 2026. 3.5% set-aside (~$32.5M) for tribal healthcare transformation; tribal proposals due February 28, 2026. Most programs expected to launch in 2027, reach full scale by 2028-2029, and hit target outcomes by December 2030. Five initiatives: rural access expansion through technology, innovative care delivery, workforce training/recruitment/retention, evidence-based prevention, infrastructure/partnerships. No RFP or procurement posted on Idaho Division of Purchasing website or DHW program page yet. Monitor: purchasing.idaho.gov. Contact: IdahoRHTP@dhw.idaho.gov.
programAddress: Idaho Department of Health and Welfare, 450 West State Street, 4th Floor, Boise, ID 83720

leadAgency: Illinois Department of Healthcare and Family Services (HFS)
notes: Illinois received $193.4M for FY2026. HFS applied for $1B total ($200M/year over 5 years). Three initiative categories: Transforming Rural Healthcare, Overcoming Geographic Barriers to Care, Building a Resilient Rural Workforce. HFS held 38+ stakeholder meetings, a public listening session with ~300 attendees, 120+ written comments/proposals, and 46 follow-up meetings during application development. Key strategies: hospital transformation grants, primary care/behavioral health integration, value-based payment initiatives, chronic disease prevention, EMS/mobile health/mobile crisis infrastructure, telehealth/tech innovation, scholarship/university program expansion, regional training/certification programs. HFS entering budget negotiations with CMS and continuing stakeholder engagement with grant partner entities. November 2025 RHTP Update to Illinois General Assembly (PDF) and Listening Session recording available on program page. No downstream subgrant or vendor opportunities posted yet.
programAddress: Illinois Department of Healthcare and Family Services, 201 South Grand Avenue East, Springfield, IL 62763

leadAgency: Indiana Family and Social Services Administration (FSSA) / Indiana Department of Health
notes: Indiana received $206.9M for FY2026. GROW regional grant application framework published with detailed timeline. $600M over 5 years across 8 regional coalitions ($120M annually). RFA releases March 1, 2026 with technical assistance beginning same day. TA period: March-July 2026. Applications due July 1, 2026 (no late submissions). Review/scoring: July-September 2026. Award announcements: no later than September 1, 2026. Grant period begins October 1, 2026. MUST apply as regional coalition - individual applications NOT accepted. Priority: 64 HRSA-designated rural counties + 9 partially-rural counties. Capital costs capped at 20%, EMR at 5%. 11-member Regional Committees per region expected to be announced February 2026 (not yet announced as of Feb 27). Regional data snapshots for all 8 regions and statewide/regional presentation decks available on the regional grants sub-page. State-managed application portal expected by March 1. Regional Grants page: in.gov/grow-rural-health/regional-grants/. Contact: GrowRuralHealth@health.in.gov.
programAddress: Indiana Family and Social Services Administration, 402 West Washington Street, Indianapolis, IN 46204

leadAgency: Iowa Department of Health and Human Services
notes: Iowa received $209.0M for FY2026. FIRST STATE IN THE NATION to award RHTP vendor funding. On Jan 30, 2026, Governor Reynolds announced $78.6M+ in competitive RFP awards. Seven RFPs now fully awarded: (1) RFP PHTHORC26009 - Best and Brightest Medical Equipment ($66M), awarded; (2) RFP PHTHORC26010 - Best and Brightest Rural Healthcare Workforce ($12.6M, up to $120K/provider), awarded to 70+ organizations; (3) RFP PHTHORC26008 - Centers of Excellence, awarded; (4) RFP PHTHOCC26755 - Combat Cancer Prevention and Screening, awarded; (5) RFP COMPADM26001 - Combat Cancer Technical Assistance Provider, awarded; (6) RFP COMPADM26002 - Health Hub Technical Assistance Provider, awarded (University of Iowa and Iowa Primary Care Association among awardees); (7) RFP COMPADM26003 - Communities of Care Technical Assistance Provider, awarded. All seven RFPs now closed and awarded. ROUND 2 COMING: Three Notices of Intent to Release posted on Iowa HHS funding page for upcoming RFPs: PHTHORC26011 Best and Brightest Equipment (Round 2), PHTHOCC26756 Combat Cancer Health Hubs, PHTHORC26012 Best and Brightest Providers (Round 2). These Round 2 RFPs not yet open for applications. Additional RFPs for other Healthy Hometowns initiatives expected. Health Hub Awardees anticipated to start Oct 1, 2026. Contact: healthyhometowns@hhs.iowa.gov or John McMullen (515) 416-8565.
programAddress: Iowa Department of Health and Human Services, Lucas Building, 321 East 12th Street, Des Moines, IA 50319

leadAgency: Kansas Department of Health and Environment (KDHE)
notes: Kansas received $221.9M for FY2026 (6th highest award). CMS approved Kansas' annual $221.89M budget on Feb 17, 2026. KDHE leads with KDADS, Kansas Rural Health Innovation Alliance (KRHIA), and University of Kansas Care Collaborative as key partners. KDHE posted first two RHTP grant RFAs on Feb 26, 2026: (1) Regional Partnership Grant Program (RPGP) — $44M available, deadline April 3, 2026, for collaborative arrangements promoting preventive care, workforce development, provider efficiency, value-based care, and innovative technologies; (2) Rural Emergency Hospital Conversion/Transformative Capital Investment (REH/CAP) — $15M available, deadline March 20, 2026, for facility renovations and capital investments. Eligible applicants include Kansas hospitals, physician practices, solo practitioners, rural health clinics, FQHCs, local health departments, tribal health facilities, behavioral health clinics, substance-use disorder facilities, and rural long-term care facilities. KDHE applicant webinar scheduled March 6, 2026 at 9:30 AM. KRHIA virtual meeting Feb 27, 2026 (livestreamed on KDHE YouTube). University of Kansas Health System Care Collaborative assisting with grant administration. RFA documents posted on KDHE RHTP program page. Contact: RHTP@ks.gov.
programAddress: Kansas Department of Health and Environment, 1000 SW Jackson Street, Suite 540, Topeka, KS 66612

leadAgency: Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services (CHFS) / Dept for Public Health
notes: Kentucky received $212.9M for FY2026 under 'Team Kentucky Rural Health Transformation.' Five interrelated initiatives planned: (1) Rural Community Hubs for Chronic Care Innovation, (2) PoWERing Maternal and Infant Health, (3) Rapid Response to Recovery (EmPATH Model), (4) Rooted in Health: Rural Dental Access, (5) Crisis to Care: Integrated EMS Response and Coordination. Regional funds will be awarded via competitive RFA with committee scoring/oversight, following Kentucky procurement law. Both for-profit and nonprofit vendors anticipated to be engaged. Led by RHTP Grant Director within HFS with Executive Oversight and State Steering Committees. Key infrastructure includes: 24/7 Medical Operations Coordination Center, EMR referral module connecting ~450 rural entities to HIE. No RFA/RFP timeline announced yet - solicitations to be publicly posted as opportunities develop. Grant spans five budget periods aligned with FY2026-FY2030. Monitor: ruralhealthplan.ky.gov and finance.ky.gov/eProcurement.
programAddress: Cabinet for Health and Family Services, 275 East Main Street, Frankfort, KY 40621

leadAgency: Louisiana Department of Health (LDH)
notes: Louisiana received $208.4M for FY2026 (among highest nationally, top 3 in Southeast). Julie Foster Hagan named Executive Director for RHTP. January 2026 notification target has been missed - no funding opportunity notifications posted as of Feb 20, 2026. LDH program page still shows January 2026 target for 'LDH funding opportunity notifications' but no notifications have been released. LDH program page lists eligible rural health facility types (rural health clinics, FQHCs, community mental health centers, opioid treatment programs, CCBHCs, Critical Access Hospitals, Sole Community Hospitals, Medicare-dependent, Low-volume, Rural Emergency Hospitals). Focus areas: chronic/complex conditions, behavioral health, perinatal/postpartum, cancer disparities, workforce, infrastructure (telehealth, EHR), paramedicine, medically tailored meal programs ('food FARMacies' with rural hospitals providing nutrition education and direct access to fresh food via local farmers). Nearly 1.1M rural residents targeted; 37% on Medicaid. Funds must be obligated by end of FY following allocation. Contact: RHTP@la.gov.
programName: Rural Health Transformation and Sustainability Program
programAddress: Louisiana Department of Health, 628 N. 4th Street, Baton Rouge, LA 70802

leadAgency: Maine Department of Health and Human Services
notes: Maine received $190.0M for FY2026. Award period: Dec 29, 2025 through Oct 30, 2026. Five focus areas: expand access to care, improve population health, strengthen rural health workforce, advance technology/data integration, promote long-term sustainability. DHHS engaging with CMS to finalize budget and begin implementation. Public information webinars were held Sept 23-24, 2025; recordings available. 'Transforming Rural Health' initiative page (maine.gov/dhhs/initiatives/transforming-rural-health) has historical context. Plan summary PDF and project narrative PDF available on program page. No downstream RFA, RFP, or subgrant application has been posted yet. Contact: DHHS at (207) 287-3707.
programAddress: Maine Department of Health and Human Services, 109 Capitol Street, 11 State House Station, Augusta, ME 04333

leadAgency: Maryland Department of Health
notes: Maryland received $168.2M for FY2026. Program page updated Feb 13, 2026, explicitly states upcoming 'opportunities to apply for the RHTP Committee and to submit proposals for competitive subrecipient funding.' RHT Committee application deadline was extended to Jan 30, 2026; committee members being selected to ensure representation from rural Maryland, prioritizing residents and rural-serving public/non-profit entities. RHTP Health Committee Briefing presented to Maryland General Assembly (Feb 2026 PDF available on mgaleg.maryland.gov). Currently in discussion with CMS regarding approved programs and adjusted budget. Two funding categories planned: (1) Immediate Impact Funds for shovel-ready activities, (2) Transformation Funds for longer-term competitive initiatives. Three initiatives: Transform Rural Health Workforce, Promote Sustainable Access and Innovative Care, Empower Rural Marylanders to Eat for Health. Sept 2025 RFI received 325+ responses. Known subrecipients include state agencies (Agriculture, Emergency Management, Labor, Housing), Rural Maryland Council, CRISP, local health departments, local EMS, and Maryland AHEC. Additional subrecipients to be determined through competitive Transformation Fund processes. Sign up for updates: surveymonkey.com/r/2026RHTP. Contact: mdh.transformruralhealth@maryland.gov.
programAddress: Maryland Department of Health, 201 W. Preston Street, Baltimore, MD 21201

leadAgency: Massachusetts Executive Office of Health and Human Services (EOHHS)
notes: Massachusetts received $162.0M for FY2026 (first year of five-year program, 2026-2030). EOHHS led interagency team including DPH, MassHealth, Office of Rural Health, Executive Office of Economic Development's Office of Rural Affairs. Seven initiative areas planned including: Population Health Advancement (technology platform connecting clinical/social/community groups, data platform, remote patient monitoring, home visiting, hospital-at-home), Innovation in Care Models (mobile health units, telehealth expansion for pharmacy/dental/behavioral health, 'Rural Digital Health Sandbox' with Mass eHealth Institute, maternal health, opioid treatment expansion), Workforce Development (rural talent recruitment, training networks, NP residencies, housing pathways for clinical staff, virtual training platform), and Capital Updates (rural hospitals, primary care, nursing facilities). Future engagement through Community Advisory Council, initiative-specific workgroups, and public information sessions - dates TBD. No downstream RFA, RFP, or subgrant application has been posted yet.
programAddress: Executive Office of Health and Human Services, One Ashburton Place, 11th Floor, Boston, MA 02108

leadAgency: Michigan Department of Health and Human Services (MDHHS)
notes: Michigan received $173.1M for FY2026 (CMS approved all activities at reduced scale). MDHHS launched RHTP Listserv for program updates - subscribers will be first to know when GFO (Grant Funding Opportunity), application materials, and technical assistance resources become available. Subscribe at MDHHS-RHTP@michigan.gov. Rural Health Transformation Advisory Council applications CLOSED (deadline was Jan 23, 2026); ~20-member council will convene quarterly to support program implementation. Council members include rural health providers, hospitals, behavioral health orgs, tribal partners, and community residents. MDHHS will administer a series of grant programs; grants available once MDHHS receives final budget approval from CMS. MHA released RHTP FAQ document (Feb 2026). Four key initiative areas: Transforming Rural Health Through Partnerships (regional care coordination hubs), Workforce for Wellness, Interoperability in Action (Health IT), Care Closer to Home Blueprint. Contact: MDHHS-RHTP@michigan.gov.
programAddress: Michigan Department of Health and Human Services, 333 S. Grand Ave, P.O. Box 30195, Lansing, MI 48909

leadAgency: Minnesota Department of Health
notes: Minnesota received $193.1M for FY2026. Grants page: health.state.mn.us/facilities/ruralhealth/ruraltrans/grants.html. Organizations may apply for funding through an allocation and/or through a competitive process, depending on eligibility. Funding for rural health organizations will be released in 2026. MDH issued HIT Advisory Services RFP (for consultant to assist up to 150 provider organizations/year with health IT adoption) - contract term April 1, 2026 - March 31, 2028, with 3-year extension option. Questions deadline was Feb 12, 2026. Eligible subgrantees include rural hospitals, rural clinics, FQHCs, CCBHCs, community mental health centers, rural Tribal nations, and other rural organizations in patient care. MDH will share more information about budget, sub-awards, and NOFOs as it becomes available. FAQ document available at health.state.mn.us/facilities/ruralhealth/ruraltrans/docs/rhtpfaq.pdf. Implementation engagement began early 2026. Contact: rural.transformation.mdh@state.mn.us.
programAddress: Minnesota Department of Health, Office of Rural Health and Primary Care, P.O. Box 64975, St. Paul, MN 55164-0975

leadAgency: Mississippi State Department of Health
notes: Mississippi received $205.9M for FY2026. Legislature pushing HB1067 requiring competitive bidding for all RHTP vendors/subcontractors - PASSED the House unanimously (121-1) on Feb 4, 2026; referred to Senate Public Health & Welfare Committee on Feb 13, 2026 (50% progression). Bill would require competitive bid process for vendors providing goods/services, including statewide health needs assessment, medical equipment, workforce programming, and health information system design. Would prioritize subgrantees in rural areas and those not receiving similar appropriations in past 5 years. Senate companion SB2570 (Mississippi Rural Regional Health Authority Act of 2026) would create Delta Regional Health Authority. Both House and Senate committees would require state agencies distributing RHTP grants to submit quarterly reports to Legislature. Governor's office overseeing fund distribution. RFQ posted (RFx# 3120003272) for accounting, auditing, and third-party administration consulting services for RHTP oversight/compliance. Statewide assessment of rural health needs projected complete by September 2026, which will inform funding decisions. Six initiatives planned. No provider RFA/RFP posted yet.
programAddress: Mississippi Division of Medicaid, 550 High Street, Suite 1000, Jackson, MS 39201

leadAgency: Missouri Department of Social Services
notes: Missouri received $216.3M for FY2026 (9th largest, top-ten ranked application nationally). Detailed procurement roadmap published on dedicated Rural Health page. Upcoming opportunities: Social Care Referral Platform/Community Information Exchange RFI (Q1 2026), Ambient AI RFI (Q1 2026), Remote Patient Technology feedback (Q2 2026), Social Care Referral Platform/CIE RFP (Q3 2026). As of Feb 27, Q1 2026 RFIs have not yet been posted on either the Rural Health page or DSS MHD RFI page - potentially overdue. Per Feb 23 Missouri Independent article, first funding period runs Dec 29, 2026 to Oct 30, 2027, with all funds needing contracts by end of first year. Largest budget item: 'digital backbone' for data sharing ($364M over 5 years). Program envisions 7 regional coordinating networks and up to 30 community hubs (2-5 counties each). Plans to hire 100+ people (90 for hubs, 20-25 for regional networks). Plans for 10 hospital infrastructure grants and 3 new service grants (oncology/obstetric surgery) in 2026. Tech contracts and staffing for new Rural Health Transformation Office also on 2026 agenda. DSS contact email: DSS.RHTP@dss.mo.gov. ToRCH FAQs page: mydss.mo.gov/torch-faqs. Monitor mydss.mo.gov/mhd/rural-health for new opportunities.
programName: ToRCH Care (Transformation of Rural Community Health Care)
programAddress: Missouri Department of Social Services, MO HealthNet Division, 615 Howerton Court, P.O. Box 6500, Jefferson City, MO 65102-6500

leadAgency: Montana Department of Public Health and Human Services (DPHHS)
notes: Montana received $233.5M for FY2026 (4th highest, up to $1.2B over 5 years). CMS issued final budget approval on February 19, 2026. First Stakeholder Advisory Committee meeting held Jan 22, 2026 at MSU with 600+ participants; SAC meets bi-annually. VENDOR WEBINAR: March 11, 2026, 10am-noon MT via Zoom — DPHHS will present five core initiatives and anticipated procurements, followed by Q&A. Registration: mt-gov.zoom.us/webinar/register. NEW VENDOR INFO PAGE launched (dphhs.mt.gov/.../InformationforVendors) with FAQ document, Interested Vendor Checklist (created Feb 23), eMACS vendor portal registration links, commodity codes guide, and event response instructions. Vendors must register in eMACS portal to respond to RFPs. Procurement timeline: RFPs expected after March 11 webinar via eMACS/bids.mt.gov; April-June 2026 anticipated partner contract awards and funding distribution begins. Most initiatives via competitive bidding; some via government-to-government contracts or grant applications. Direct funding announcements at ruralhealth.mt.gov. State will prefer smaller number of statewide partners rather than individual providers. Rural Health Center of Excellence (CoE) planned to provide data-backed, tailored recommendations for facility sustainability. Five core initiatives: Workforce, Sustainable Access, Innovative Care, Community Health & Prevention, Technology Innovation. Eligible: tribal nations, government agencies, health care providers, educational institutions, nonprofits. DPHHS actively hiring 10+ RHTP staff: Program Director, 5 Program Managers (one per initiative), Grants Manager, Budget Analyst, Compliance Officer, Communications Officer, DLI Liaison; positions for Community Engagement, Tribal Liaisons, Research/Data Scientists, and EHR Integration Specialists coming soon. All positions term-limited through Sept 30, 2030. Must submit initial federal progress report by August 2026; must distribute and start spending 2026 grant by September 2026. No RFPs posted on eMACS yet as of March 3. MHA partner interest form: cvent.me/z41XXV. Contact: HHSRuralHealthTransformation@mt.gov.
programAddress: Montana Department of Public Health and Human Services, P.O. Box 4210, Helena, MT 59604-4210

leadAgency: Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services
notes: Nebraska received $218.5M for FY2026. Revised budgets submitted to CMS Jan 30, 2026 with 30-day review period (increased award amount of additional $18.5M required revised application). CMS must approve before funds are distributed and work can begin - earliest approval ~March 2026. State RHTP page now confirms RFAs starting in March 2026, with additional RFAs throughout each funding year. Funding awards expected Spring 2026. Inaugural CMS Rural Health Summit scheduled March 2026 (during CMS Quality Conference) to promote interstate collaboration. Seven integrated initiatives: (1) Food as Medicine (school kitchen modernization, food pantries, farm-to-school), (2) Regionalized Rural Access and Navigation, (3) Rural Workforce Acceleration, (4) eHealth and Mobile Care, (5) Rural Emergency Behavioral Health, (6) Assisted Living Facility Special Needs Population Incentive, (7) Rural Health Technology Catalyst Fund. Nebraska's full budget proposal details 35 projects costing $885M over five years. GovDelivery notification signup available: public.govdelivery.com/accounts/NESTATE/subscriber/new?topic_id=NESTATE_834. Monitor dedicated RHTP page and DHHS Grants and Contract Opportunities page. Contact: DHHS.RHTP@nebraska.gov.
programAddress: Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services, 301 Centennial Mall South, Lincoln, NE 68508

leadAgency: Nevada Health Authority
notes: Nevada received $179.9M for FY2026. First application opportunities to be announced starting in March 2026 via NVHA website and ListServ. RHTSC Steering Committee application closed Jan 19, 2026; approved members to be announced soon. First Steering Committee meeting: March 11, 2026 at 1:00 PM. First public workshop held Feb 18, 2026 (presentation slides available on RHTP page); second workshop scheduled March 5, 2026 at 1:30 PM. Winter Stakeholder Update Workshop held Jan 27. Nevada Rural Hospital Partners met Feb 20 to discuss strategic impact and path forward. 2026 RHT Project Timeline PDF now available on RHTP page. ATI Advisory reports RHIT Grant RFP will be released May 2026, with awards in September 2026 (contracts Sept 15, 2026 - Sept 30, 2027). NVHA actively hiring program staff (Management Analyst III and IV positions). Four funded initiatives with allocations: RHOAP virtual specialty mentorship/RPM/AI (15%, ~$27M/yr), Flex Fund for rural provider infrastructure (20%, ~$36M/yr), Workforce Recruitment and Rural Access (40%, ~$72M/yr), Rural Health Innovation and Technology Grant (15%, ~$27M/yr). Strong preference for awards to rural CAHs, CCBHCs, clinics, FQHCs, school-based health centers. Sign up for NVHA ListServ for announcements. Contact: RHTP@nvha.nv.gov.
programAddress: Nevada Health Authority, 9850 Double R Blvd, Reno, NV 89521

leadAgency: New Hampshire Department of Health and Human Services
notes: New Hampshire received $204.0M for FY2026, the largest award among New England states. Governor Ayotte established GO-NORTH (Governor's Office of New Opportunities & Rural Transformational Health) to oversee implementation. Donnalee Lozeau (former Nashua mayor/Southern NH Services CEO) appointed Director in Jan 2026. GO-NORTH is an independent office within the Governor's office embedded in DHHS. Legislative budget committee approved funding for GO-NORTH to hire ~20 staff, with first-year budget including ~$550K classified state employees and ~$316K unclassified employees. ~$121M designated for grants in initial year. Critical spending constraint: 18 months to spend first tranche; subsequent years have 24-month spending windows. NH DHHS CFO Nathan White emphasized tight deadline driving procurement process. State will issue 'a series of requests for proposals' to allow nonprofits and other organizations to compete for grant funding - timeline not yet specified. 'Granite Strong. Future Ready.' plan focuses on preventive health, sustainable access, workforce, innovative payment models, technology/digital health. Over 300 stakeholders contributed input. No RHTP-specific RFPs posted yet on DHHS open RFPs page. Monitor: dhhs.nh.gov RHTP page and Open RFPs page. Contact: RHTF@dhhs.nh.gov.
programAddress: New Hampshire Department of Health and Human Services, 129 Pleasant Street, Concord, NH 03301

leadAgency: New Jersey Department of Health
notes: New Jersey received $147.3M for FY2026 (smallest state award). RFA 'Rural Health Transformation Program-Advancing Technology, Prevention, and Workforce Capacity 2026' - application window CLOSED (opened Dec 30, 2025, closed Jan 20, 2026). Now in review/award phase — no award recipients publicly announced yet as of Feb 27, 2026. Rural Health Information Hub lists this funding as INACTIVE. Project/budget period: Feb 1 - Oct 30, 2026; final expenditure report due Nov 15, 2026 (extended deadline Nov 30, 2026). $27.5M distributed via four activity areas: Activity A - Rural Preventive Health ($8M, up to 16 awards of $200K-$800K), Activity B - Telehealth & RPM ($9M, up to 10 awards of $200K-$1M), Activity C - Training & Capacity Building ($500K, up to 3 awards of $50K-$250K), Activity D - Workforce Recruitment/Retention ($10M, single award). Vendor resource directory and technical assistance Q&A document published. Applied via NJ DOH SAGE IntelliGrants portal. RFA document: healthapps.nj.gov/noticeofgrant/documents/DOHP26RHT_rfa.pdf. Eligible: municipal/county governments, educational institutions, hospitals, nonprofits, clinics, CBOs, tribal organizations serving rural NJ. Contact: RHTP.Application@doh.nj.gov.
programName: New Jersey Rural Health Transformation Program
programAddress: New Jersey Department of Health, P.O. Box 360, Trenton, NJ 08625-0360

leadAgency: New Mexico Health Care Authority (HCA)
notes: New Mexico received $211.5M for FY2026. Guidehouse awarded consultant support contract for application preparation. Funding will be distributed through two primary mechanisms: direct subawards and competitive procurements/applications. Rural Health Sustainability and Innovation Center being established as central coordination hub to launch initiatives, manage subawards and procurement activities, and provide technical assistance ($123M budgeted over 5 years). Plans include $188M competitive grant program for rural, frontier, and tribal communities; $393M for regional specialty and maternal care networks; $243M for rural health workforce expansion. HCA previously issued RFP 26-630-8000-0007 for consultant support (closed Sept 17, 2025) and RFI for stakeholder input (closed Oct 10, 2025) - both were pre-application, NOT downstream vendor funding. No downstream subgrant or vendor application posted yet. Contact: Wenona.padgett@hca.nm.gov. Monitor HCA Open RFPs page (hca.nm.gov/lookingforinformation/open-rfps/).
programName: New Mexico Rural Health Transformation Program
programAddress: New Mexico Health Care Authority, P.O. Box 2348, Santa Fe, NM 87504-2348

leadAgency: New York State Department of Health
notes: New York received $212.1M for FY2026. Four initiatives: Rural Community Health Integration, Technology-Enhanced Primary Care (PCMH with AI), Rural Roots Workforce Development, Technology Innovation/Cybersecurity. January 16, 2026 presentation available on program page. DOH working with CMS to finalize cooperative agreement details; once finalized, NY will provide additional details to stakeholders around allocation of resources and funding opportunities. 47 of 62 counties classified as rural. Nearly 170 stakeholder organizations contributed to plan development. No downstream subgrant or vendor RFA posted yet. Contact: RHTP@health.ny.gov.
programName: Transforming Rural Healthcare in New York
programAddress: New York State Department of Health, Corning Tower, Empire State Plaza, Albany, NY 12237

leadAgency: North Carolina Department of Health and Human Services (NCDHHS)
notes: North Carolina received $213.0M for FY2026. NC ROOTS (Rural Organizations Orchestrating Transformation for Sustainability) Hub Lead RFA# 2026-NCROOTS is NOW OPEN. Deadline: 5:00 PM EST on April 2, 2026. Up to six Hub Lead organizations will be selected, one per NC Medicaid Standard Plan region. Applicants must demonstrate infrastructure, knowledge, experience, and capacity to serve as both programmatic and fiduciary lead for their region. Application materials available: RFA document, Applicant Budget Worksheet, Budget Worksheet Instructions, RFA Application form, and PDF Navigation Guide. Feb 26, 2026 virtual information webinar recording available. Hub Leads will provide centralized governance and support for network partners including rural hospitals, FQHCs, and private physician practices. Hub Lead selection and onboarding: April-October 2026. Six strategic focus areas: Community-Rooted Care Networks, Prevention/Chronic Disease Management, Behavioral Health Expansion, Workforce Development, Financial Sustainability, Technology/Innovation. 100% of budget period 1 costs funded with federal funds. Jan 16, 2026 town hall and Feb 26, 2026 webinar recordings available. Contact: monitor ncdhhs.gov RHTP Grant Opportunities page.
programName: NC ROOTS (Rural Organizations Orchestrating Transformation for Sustainability)
programAddress: NC Department of Health and Human Services, 2001 Mail Service Center, Raleigh, NC 27699-2000

applicationOpenDate: Pending CMS approval of revised budget
leadAgency: North Dakota Health and Human Services
notes: North Dakota received $198.9M for FY2026 ($500M+ over 5 years). KEY UPDATE: Per program funding page (updated Feb 2026), no funding opportunities are currently open. ND lawmakers approved RHTP funding, but the state is now waiting for CMS to approve the revised RHTP budget submitted on January 30, 2026. CMS has 30 days to respond. Once approved, funding opportunities will be released in phases across four pillars: (1) Strengthen and Stabilize Rural Health Workforce - Coming Soon; (2) Make North Dakota Healthy Again (Preventive Care, Eating Healthy) - Coming Soon; (3) Bring High-Quality Health Care Closer to Home - Coming Soon; (4) Connecting Technology, Data and Providers for a Stronger ND - Coming Soon. Legislature approved $398M for first two years via special session (bills signed by Governor Armstrong). Budget: $116M access, $33.4M technology, $32.2M workforce, $17.1M healthy lifestyles. Short application turnaround expected - must commit funds by Oct 2026. Listening sessions, TA calls, and outreach activities being planned. Broad eligibility: providers, faith communities, schools, others. New vendor registration guidance posted for those wanting to apply. Three positions currently open: Rural Health Tribal Relations Manager, Rural Health Managers, Coordinators. GovDelivery signup: public.govdelivery.com/accounts/NDDHHS/subscriber/new?topic_id=NDDHHS_189. Contact: rhtp@nd.gov.
programName: North Dakota Rural Health Transformation Program
programAddress: North Dakota Health and Human Services, 600 East Boulevard Ave., Bismarck, ND 58505-0250

leadAgency: Ohio Department of Health
notes: Ohio received $202.0M for FY2026. Solicitation reference documents page: odh.ohio.gov/know-our-programs/rural-health-transformation-program/solicitation-reference-documents. ACTIVE VENDOR RFP: 'Rural Health Workforce Pipeline; Pathways Upskilling and Retention' (SRC0000036107). Posted Jan 2, 2026, deadline March 10, 2026 at 3:00 PM EST. Questions due Feb 27, 2026. Estimated $5M-$15M annually, up to $60M total contract value. Contract period: April 1, 2026 - June 30, 2027 with renewals through June 30, 2031. Scope: statewide workforce pipeline strategy across high schools, career tech, community colleges, universities, and employers in 73 rural counties. Includes apprenticeships, CHW and pharmacist training, recruitment incentives, 6-month housing stipends for 5-year rural service commitments. One award anticipated. ODH Director Dr. Bruce Vanderhoff presented to Ohio House Health Committee on Feb 25, 2026, detailing funding allocation: $92M for Rural Health Innovation Hubs/Clinically Integrated Networks, $21.1M for rural school-based health centers, $13.5M for workforce programs. Vanderhoff encouraged 'grant proposals from groups looking to do more innovative work.' Pharmacy services grants also open alongside worker training. Additional RFPs for other pillars (Innovation Hubs, Emergency Care, School-Based Health Centers, OH SEE Expansion) expected - ODH website states 'watch for additional RFPs.' Submit via OhioBuys.
programAddress: Ohio Department of Health, 246 N. High Street, Columbus, OH 43215

leadAgency: Oklahoma State Department of Health
notes: Oklahoma received $223.5M for FY2026 (one of highest allocations). CMS has now released ~$202M of the $223.5M award, with $21M remaining under review. Feb 12, 2026 RHT Program Touchpoint Webinar held (recording & presentation available on program page). Competitive RFPs/NOFOs will be posted as program details are finalized. Some initiatives delegated to partner agencies (OHCA, Dept of Education, Healthcare Workforce Training Commission) which may issue own procurements. State plans microgrants for local communities to apply for wellness-focused funding (no launch date yet). Rural definition expanded from 59 to 75 eligible counties (all towns/communities under ~50K population outside Oklahoma and Tulsa counties). Co-directors: Lisa Rother (health systems) and Jackie Kanak (operations/population health). Listening sessions planned across state over next year. Oklahoma Rise 25 in 25 RHTP Task Force launched (independent think tank at MCOK Innovation Lab) to accelerate implementation. Initiatives include behavioral health integration, community health worker expansion, rural residency programs, EHR expansion, school-based services, chronic disease management, and telestroke. Monitor: oklahoma.gov/health/rhtp.html, Oklahoma Central Purchasing, and OHCA Procurement pages. Contact: OklahomaRHTP@health.ok.gov.
programAddress: Oklahoma State Department of Health, 123 Robert S. Kerr Ave., Suite 1702, Oklahoma City, OK 73102-6406

leadAgency: Oregon Health Authority (OHA)
notes: Oregon received $197.3M for FY2026. Feb 24, 2026 webinar held (two identical sessions at 11am PT) to help organizations prepare for grant proposal process - recordings and slide deck posted on program page. OHA preparing updated FAQs responding to submitted questions. Three funding distribution mechanisms: (1) Catalyst Awards - competitive RFGP for ready-to-implement projects across three initiatives (Healthy Communities & Prevention, Workforce Capacity & Resilience, Technology & Data Modernization); (2) Immediate Impact Awards - direct/non-competitive for state-identified opportunities; (3) Regional Sustainability Awards - subcontractors for rural health network facilitation/TA. RFGP release Spring 2026, response deadline early summer, awards mid-2026. Must serve rural (10+ miles from 40K+ population center) or frontier (6 or fewer people/sq mi). Target populations: maternal/child health, behavioral health, aging in place, chronic disease. No limits on entity types. Contact: RHTP@oha.oregon.gov or (971) 446-0946.
programAddress: Oregon Health Authority, 500 Summer Street NE, E-20, Salem, OR 97301-1097

leadAgency: Pennsylvania Department of Human Services
notes: Pennsylvania received $193.3M for FY2026. $200M/year x 5 years = $1B total. Funds flowing through eight Regional Rural Care Collaboratives (RCCs) built on existing PREP organizations, PA AHEC, University of Pittsburgh Medicaid Research Center, and other partners. PA has statutory authority via Act 45 of 2025 (Article II-H) establishing the program within DHS. February 2026 update presentation published on program page (2026-02-06). Despite $193M award, rural hospital closure concerns persist as Medicaid cuts may offset gains (per Feb 15, 2026 Spotlight PA/WESA report). Six priority areas: technology/telehealth, workforce, maternal health, behavioral health, older adults, emergency services/transportation. No specific downstream RFA or vendor application posted yet. Monitor DHS program page for updates.
programAddress: Pennsylvania Department of Human Services, P.O. Box 2675, Harrisburg, PA 17105-2675

leadAgency: Rhode Island Department of Health (RIDOH) / EOHHS
notes: Rhode Island received $156.2M for FY2026. Interest form available at https://redcap.link/bdi3kicv to join listserv for program updates. EOHHS Rural Health Transformation Grant Initiative page (eohhs.ri.gov/initiatives/rural-health-transformation-grant) has application documents (project narrative, one-page summary, Governor's endorsement letter, letters of support) but no downstream vendor RFA/RFP yet. Five strategic goals: Making Rural America Healthy Again, Sustainable Access, Workforce Development, Innovative Care, Tech Innovation. Planned investments include Health IT modernization grants for digital tools/telehealth/AI, workforce development with new clinical training placements, mobile health services, statewide tele-dentistry triage system, and EMS modernization. Eligible rural areas: New Shoreham (Block Island), Westerly, and 18 state-defined rural towns. RHTP-specific contact: OHHS.RIRHTP@ohhs.ri.gov. General contact: RIDOH.OPCRH@health.ri.gov. Monitor EOHHS page for downstream opportunity announcements.
programName: Rhode Island Rural Health Transformation Program
programAddress: Rhode Island Department of Health, 3 Capitol Hill, Providence, RI 02908

leadAgency: South Carolina Department of Health and Human Services
notes: South Carolina received $200.0M for FY2026. Dedicated RHTP page: scdhhs.gov/rhtp (contains application materials, webinar recordings and slides from Sept and Nov 2025 webinars). Five major initiatives: improving chronic disease outcomes, maternal health, 'Connections to Care' digital infrastructure (EHR, RPM, telehealth, statewide resource database), Tech Catalyst Fund for rural health technology startups, workforce development. SCDHHS will announce funding opportunities via Medicaid bulletins - providers should sign up on SCDHHS website. No downstream RFA, RFP, or subgrant application has been posted yet. Contact: grants@scdhhs.gov or info@scdhhs.gov.
programName: South Carolina Rural Health Transformation Program
programAddress: South Carolina Department of Health and Human Services, P.O. Box 8206, Columbia, SC 29202-8206

leadAgency: South Dakota Department of Health
notes: South Dakota received $189.5M for FY2026. Governor Rhoden signed HB 1044 into law on Jan 29, 2026. FIRST ROUND OF RFPs NOW OPEN: Announced February 25, 2026, seven initiatives seeking proposals via the SD posting board (postingboard.esmsolutions.com): (1) Digital Health Modernization and Technology Grant Program Manager - support statewide digital health system modernization; (2) Medicaid Primary Accountable Care Transformation (PACT) - vendor support for population-based alternative payment methodology; (3) Department of Social Services Project Manager - cross-agency coordination role; (4) Chronic Disease Management Initiative Project Manager - liaison and reporting support; (5) Caregiver Support Programming - services, education, and resources for caregivers; (6) Chronic Disease Self-Management Programming - expansion in rural and Western SD; (7) Expand and Strengthen the Rural Community Health Worker Workforce - CHW/CHR workforce expansion via TA and professional development. DOH Secretary Melissa Magstadt stated this 'first round of RFPs provides opportunities for partners to support implementation efforts through key leadership and project management roles.' Awards expected between March and May 2026. More initiatives expected as the project continues to roll out. Major programmatic RFPs and RFAs for Spring 2026 (March-May) still expected per earlier timeline. Ten strategic initiative areas with one-pagers available on program page. Contact: DOH.info@state.sd.us or (605) 773-3361.
programName: South Dakota Rural Health Transformation Project
programAddress: South Dakota Department of Health, 600 East Capitol Avenue, Pierre, SD 57501-2536

leadAgency: Tennessee Department of Health
notes: Tennessee received $206.9M for FY2026 ($1B over 5 years). Joint Ways and Means Committee voted unanimously Jan 14, 2026 to accept federal funds. Program page now states: 'Check this Page in the Upcoming Weeks for Information on Competitive Opportunities.' Existing Healthcare Resiliency Program (HRP) at tn.gov/health/hrp.html awards competitive grants for Capital Investments and Practice Transformation - likely model for RHTP distribution. Legislation being introduced in 2026 to eliminate certificate of need and enable full scope of practice (prerequisites tied to RHTP funding). Contact: Rural.Health@tn.gov. Monitor TN DOH rural health page and Central Procurement Office.
programAddress: Tennessee Department of Health, 710 James Robertson Parkway, Nashville, TN 37243

leadAgency: Texas Health and Human Services Commission (HHSC)
notes: Texas received $281.3M for FY2026, the LARGEST state award ($1.4B over 5 years). 'Rural Texas Strong' six-part strategy. IMPORTANT: HHSC 'is not meeting with potential vendors or applicants to maintain fairness for all parties' - formal quiet period in effect. Budget Period 1 revised documents (Budget Narrative, Project Narrative, Org Chart) submitted to CMS January 30, 2026. Six initiatives with specific procurement types: (1) Make Rural Texans Healthy Again - direct awards to rural hospital districts; (2) Rural Texas Patients in Driver's Seat - RFP for 2+ clinically integrated networks/ACOs (60%+ rural members); (3) Lone Star Advanced AI & Telehealth - RFP for 2+ clinically integrated networks/ACOs; (4) Next Generation Small Town Doctor - RFA for rural healthcare providers (at least 1 award per rural county); (5) Unified Care Infrastructure & Cyber - RFO for TX DIR-listed Managed Security Providers; (6) Infrastructure & Capital Improvement - RFA for rural hospitals, clinics, behavioral health, EMS, pharmacies. Per program page, eligible applicants include local governments, rural hospitals, rural FQHCs, rural behavioral health providers, and other qualified applicants who will be eligible to apply 'in the spring.' No Rural Texas Strong solicitations on HHSC RFA page or ESBD as of Feb 27, 2026. Community-driven wellness solutions can begin right away; contract solicitations for Y2 funding being developed simultaneously. Subscribe via GovDelivery or monitor Electronic State Business Daily (ESBD). Contact: RuralTexasStrong@hhs.texas.gov.
programName: Rural Texas Strong: Supporting Health and Wellness
programAddress: Texas Health and Human Services Commission, 4601 W. Guadalupe St., Austin, TX 78751

leadAgency: Utah Department of Health and Human Services
notes: Utah received $195.7M for FY2026 ($1B over 5 years). DHHS previously stated plans to issue multiple RFPs 'as early as January 2026' with short submission timelines - this target has slipped. Feb 12, 2026 informational webinar held (12:30-1:00 PM MT) covering Year 1 funding plan and development updates since the Dec 29 Notice of Award. Webinar recording posted on program page. No RFPs have been posted yet as of Feb 27, 2026 - program page emphasizes community readiness: 'Rural partners can convene, assess capacity and gaps, identify shared priorities, establish formal collaborations.' No RHTP RFPs found on DHHS Purchasing/Procurement portal. Seven outcome-driven initiatives with itemized budgets: PATH ($155M health/wellness), RISE ($187M workforce), SHIFT/FAST/LIFT ($437M innovation/access), SUPPORT/LINCS ($190M technology). Projects must be implementation-ready and cannot create ongoing state financial obligations. Rural definition excludes Salt Lake, Davis, Utah, and Weber counties. RFPs could appear any week - short turnaround expected when they drop. Contact: ruralhealth@utah.gov.
programAddress: Utah Department of Health and Human Services, 195 North 1950 West, Salt Lake City, UT 84116

leadAgency: Vermont Agency of Human Services
notes: Vermont received $195.1M for FY2026 (among highest per-capita awards). AHS announced it will 'roll out RFPs and procurement options, some as grants and some as contracts.' AHS RHT Program team has created a listserv for interested parties - sign up link available on healthcarereform.vermont.gov. Plans to post full application and summary on website and conduct listening webinars for prospective applicants. Jan 8, 2026 presentation to House Appropriations Committee detailed program overview and next steps. Chittenden County classified as non-rural for grant purposes; providers from non-rural counties may apply for projects demonstrably impacting rural communities. Compressed spending window - must spend by September with reporting 60 days before year-end, creating urgency for rapid procurement. Award is 1 year at a time - second-year funding not guaranteed. Revised budget submitted to CMS by Jan 30. Five initiative areas: Regionalization and Innovative Care Strategies, Clinically Integrated Network of Shared Services, Strengthening Primary Care, Healthcare Workforce Development, Price Transparency and Insurance Competition. Monitor AHS website, DVHA RFP page (dvha.vermont.gov/administration/requests-proposals), and sign up for listserv.
programAddress: Vermont Agency of Human Services, 280 State Drive, Center Building, Waterbury, VT 05671-1080

leadAgency: Virginia Department of Medical Assistance Services (DMAS)
notes: Virginia received $189.5M for FY2026 under 'VA Rural Vitality' initiative. DMAS hiring core team staff in early 2026; recruiting for Director, Rural Health Transformation (RHT) position in Richmond. Implementation support contractors will help launch 'ready-to-launch programs' first. Year One prioritizing ready-to-launch programs, with additional initiatives designed and introduced in subsequent years. Four initiatives: CareIQ ($280M+ in health IT over 5 years including EHR upgrades, RPM, digital health innovation), Homegrown Health Heroes (medical residencies at rural hospitals, healthcare apprenticeships, high school/community college pathways), Connected Care Closer to Home (mobile clinics, telehealth), Live Well Together (wearables, physical activity, food-as-medicine). Six rural regions: Southwest, Roanoke/Alleghany, Southside, Northern Piedmont, Shenandoah Valley, Eastern Shore/Northern Neck. Regional updates and listening sessions planned twice yearly through 2031. Virginia Center for Health Innovation (VCHI) leading independent evaluation. UnitedHealthcare Community & State involved in implementation. Additional partners (rural hospitals, FQHCs, free clinics) to be selected competitively as program expands. Monitor: DMAS procurement and Virginia Business Opportunities (eVA) portal. Contact: monitor dmas.virginia.gov RHTP page.
programAddress: Department of Medical Assistance Services, 600 East Broad Street, Suite 1300, Richmond, VA 23219

leadAgency: Washington State Health Care Authority
notes: Washington received $181.3M for FY2026. CMS approved Washington's revised budget on Feb 10, 2026, matching the $181M allocation. Next step: review and finalize cooperative agreement with CMS. Washington's expected timeline shows first round of funds distributed October 1, 2026 (must be used by Sept 30, 2028) - substantially behind other states. Revised budget prioritizes projects that can leverage existing partnerships and are ready for rapid implementation in Year 1. Six initiatives: (1) Ignite Innovation in rural hospitals, (2) Prevent disease and manage care in community settings, (3) Invest in health of Native families, (4) Adopt technology and data solutions, (5) Develop rural workforce, (6) Expand/sustain rural behavioral health. Investment areas: hospital infrastructure, technology, maternal health, new facilities, healthcare workforce housing. Bipartisan legislative support confirmed; new rural health transformation budget approved. Tribal consultation conducted (Dear Tribal Leader Letter Sept 2025). Contact: payingforvalue@hca.wa.gov. Monitor: HCA program page for announcements.
programAddress: Washington State Health Care Authority, 626 8th Avenue SE, Olympia, WA 98501

leadAgency: West Virginia Department of Health
notes: West Virginia received $199.5M for FY2026 (nearly double guaranteed minimum). WV Department of Health appointed Carrie Jeffries (DNP, MPH) as Director and Dr. Soumya Prasad (pediatrician, 20+ years healthcare leadership) as Deputy Director on Feb 13, 2026. Also hired Michael Sheets (former state procurement officer) and John Leite (former assistant to public health commissioner) who started week of Feb 10. DOH 'aggressively hiring' remaining team members including seven Program Managers for each flagship initiative: Connected Care Grid, Rural Health Link, Mountain State Care Force, Health to Prosperity Pipeline, Personal Health Accelerator, HealthTech Appalachia, Smart Care Catalyst. All positions are 'at-will' for up to five years. LEGISLATIVE UPDATE: Make WV Healthy Act (HB 4982) passed House 91-4 on Feb 10 and Senate on Feb 19, now awaiting Governor Morrisey's signature. SB 570 (spending authorization to transfer $199.5M federal funds to DOH) passed Senate Feb 13 but STALLED in House Finance Committee as of Feb 24 — DOH expressed 'serious concern' that delay puts funding and future federal funding at risk. Crossover Day is March 4. HB 4982 establishes Office of Healthy Lifestyles, 'Food is Medicine' program via Medicaid, and related wellness initiatives. New DOH program page: health.wv.gov/rural-health-transformation-program. Funds awarded based on need and readiness with emphasis on measurable outcomes. Contact: RHTP@wv.gov.
programName: West Virginia Rural Health Transformation Program
programAddress: Office of the Governor, State Capitol, 1900 Kanawha Blvd. E, Charleston, WV 25305

leadAgency: Wisconsin Department of Health Services (DHS)
notes: Wisconsin received $203.7M for FY2026. RHTP Advisory Council application closed February 27, 2026 (survey.alchemer.com/s3/8621599/DRAFT-RHTP-Advisory-Council-Application) - advisory role, not funding. DHS Office of Grants Management (OGM) will lead implementation and oversight. DHS recruiting Program Director and additional staff; positions to be posted at dhs.wisconsin.gov/job-opps/index.htm. 2026 funds distributed in early January, may be spent through Sept 30, 2027. Three initiatives: workforce, technology/innovation, care transformation through partnerships. Plans include competitive grants for innovative workforce projects, closed-loop referral systems, telehealth, multi-sector partnership grants. No RHTP-related solicitations on DHS Current Grant Funding Opportunities page as of Feb 27, 2026. Procurement contact: DHSProcurement@dhs.wisconsin.gov. Subscribe for updates: public.govdelivery.com/accounts/WIDHS/subscriber/new?topic_id=WIDHS_75.
programName: Building a Healthier Future for Rural Wisconsin
programAddress: Wisconsin Department of Health Services, 1 W. Wilson St., Madison, WI 53703

leadAgency: Wyoming Department of Health
notes: Wyoming received $205.0M for FY2026 ($5M more than requested, second-largest per-capita award behind Alaska). HB 122 PASSED the House unanimously (57-0, with 5 absent) on Feb 19, 2026 on third reading; received by Senate for consideration (as of Feb 24). The bill establishes the WY RHTP, creates advisory committee (9 voting members appointed by governor), perpetuity fund (80% of Year 1 funds), and expenditure account. Perpetuity fund allocations: CAH Basic Incentive (40.7%), EMS Regionalization (26.9%), Workforce Education (21.6%), Physician Post-Graduate Medical Education (10.8%). Years 2-5: 69.5% to perpetuity fund. Annual distribution: greater of prior-year program income or 4% of total accumulated fund assets. Legislature budget session commenced Feb 9; session adjourns March 11. Program page updated Feb 24, 2026. Timeline: March-April 2026 RFAs and RFPs posted with informational webinars and Q&A sessions prior to posting; May-June 2026 awards made and funds drawn down; July-August 2026 contracts executed. Key elements: CAH Basic incentive program, EMS consolidation grants, medical education pipeline with 5-year service commitments. Contact: monitor health.wyo.gov RHTP page.
programAddress: Wyoming Department of Health, 2300 Capitol Ave, Cheyenne, WY 82002
